summary: The post addresses the common struggle managers face in feeling worthwhile when they're no longer the ones building things. Charity argues that managerial satisfaction is fundamentally different from engineering satisfaction — it's slower, less visible, and lacks the dopamine drip of shipping code. She offers practical frameworks for locating your impact in big-picture outcomes, small human moments, and crisis situations, while calling out the performative selflessness managers are expected to display.
key_points: New managers should expect to feel lost for at least two years and not worry about self-assessment during that period
Manager satisfaction operates on fundamentally different timelines and feedback loops than engineering work
The expectation that managers must derive all fulfillment from others' success is performative and dishonest
Managerial value accrues over time through trust-building, not through any single visible action
In a crisis, the real test is whether you're a source of calm or a situation to be managed
money_quotes: As an engineer, you get that very tactile sense of merging code, solving puzzles and incrementally pushing the business forward. It has a rhythm and a powerful drip, drip, drip of dopamine, and as a manager you will never ever feel that.
There is nothing wrong with wanting credit for your work, and affirmation and validation, and there is nothing inherently noble about not wanting those things.

Do your feelings become other people's problems? This reflects your ability to regulate your own feelings and emotional impulses under stress, and most of us quite overestimate our own power to self-regulate.
In the end, your worth as a manager is seen in the trail you leave behind.
key_insight: Managerial worth is measured not in daily visible output but in the long trail of trust, team health, and crisis resilience you build over years — and pretending you don't need recognition for that work is dishonest.
